speaker: interviewer
text: Four-- December the 6th, 1995. Survivor, Hanns Saenger, interviewer Heather Rossevitch, in Johannesburg, South Africa. Language, English. Hanns, when you arrived in South Africa, how did the South Africans-- and South African Jews, in particular-- treat you coming out of, originally, Germany, and having experienced what you experienced?

speaker: interviewee
text: The standard of pay was, if they gave you a job that paid you 5 pounds a month. And that's exactly what we all got. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] It was the exception that you were actually received in-- in-- the homes of people. I can remember two families where we certainly were invited on Shabbat evening, or-- and-- and-- were-- we were made very welcome. But [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there's not a great deal-- there was not a great deal of love lost. German Jews were considered a further threat, because by now, anti-Semitism was raising its head in South Africa.

speaker: interviewer
text: So why would they see you as a threat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you take the-- the political background of South Africa. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] When we came here, the government-- the prime minister was Hertzog, and Smuts was the deputy prime minister. And the-- the-- [PAUSES FOR 12 SECONDS] how shall I put it? It was the beginning of a political movement called the Greyshirts. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The Greyshirts were one of the three movements that developed around that time. There was the Greyshirts, there was the-- they started in October '33. They were started by a man called Louis Weichardt, who was an Afrikaner of German stock, and he founded the Greyshirts. Subsequently, they changed into a political party called the South African National Party. The Greyshirts had about 2,000 members, but they were very loud anti-Semites. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] Weichardt was a British subject. He was in Germany during the First World War, and joined to fight for Germany, and came back here in 1923. Subsequently, he tried to form a Nazi movement, and that brought about this Greyshirt movement, which never really took off-- except making a lot of noise. He was centered around Cape Town. In 1939, the Greyshirts joined the Nationalist Party, and in 1938, Eric Lowe, who was a minister in the Nationalist Party, introduced a bill in parliament prohibiting Jewish immigration. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] At the end of 1938, was the centenary of the Great Trek, and a movement called the Ossewabrandwag was started. 'Ossewabrandwag' means 'ox-wagon sentinel.' It perpetrated the nationalist fervor, and was headed by a Dr.-- Dr. Van Rensburg, a previous administrator of the free state. A great admirer of Hitler, after his visit to Germany, he formed a stormtrooper section-- which people have forgotten-- and it was called the Stormjaers. By 1941, he's alleged to have had a membership of 300,000. And the third movement of the time was Oswald Pirow. Now he called his movement the Noveau Order, and it was an anti-democratic Afrikaans nationalist group. He formed it in 1940. He was by far, intellectually, the-- the brightest, and indeed he was one of the advocates in the government team when the Rivonia Trial was on. And he died in the middle of that trial. And I think the trial might have been different if he would have been alive. He was, by far, the smartest of their team. That led in 1943 to the bus strike, 1946 to the miners' strike, and in 1950 to the Group Areas Act. So you can see how-- how the country moved to the right, and moved away from the English influence, which Smuts had always encouraged, whilst we were a member of-- of the British Commonwealth. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] The-- [PAUSES FOR 15 SECONDS] One thing that I would like to bring up that we've left out up to now, is I joined the army in 1940.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was then 21.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they call you up?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We had a voluntary army in this country. And I think in '42, Smuts wanted to send the troops up north. And for this, you had to swear a second oath, which entitled you to a red tab on your shoulder. And I said to my own commanding officer that I was prepared to sign-- to-- to swear that oath, provided I had a South African passport. But I wasn't prepared to do it if I was going to go up there-- I had no passport. And he made-- wrote a letter to General Smuts, he set up my case, and within six weeks, my naturalization came through-- as well as my father's, and my late brother's. And [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] that enabled me to swear the oath, go up north-- I went twice to Abyssinia-- and [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I came back. And then for medical reasons, I was posted into the motor transport. And in about 1943-- must have been end of '43 or early '44-- I got secret orders, signed by General Smuts, himself, to send out trucks to given addresses the following morning at 6 o'clock, to pick up an officer in each case. That officer had secret orders to pick up troops. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And we managed to arrest 600 Nazis, and the police force, from Springs to [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you arrest them? Or where did you find them?

speaker: interviewee
text: We stopped outside every police station, indicated police station, we knew exactly-- we had the names, and Smuts actually had Secret Service information that the Nazis, amongst the right-wingers, intended taking over the Brookehouse House in Commissioner Street, and the heads of the mining shafts-- which were then on the reef, still, in Johannesburg, and along the reef. And these 600 men were arrested and put into a train, because we had nowhere to put them. We didn't know what to do with them. And we kept that train in Braamfontein, outside Park Station for two nights. The paper were full of news that 600 men had been arrested. Nobody knew where they were. They were actually in the middle of Johannesburg on a train, until we emptied a military camp in [NON-ENGLISH], which became a detention camp for these Nazis.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they resist at all when you arrested them?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They were taken completely by surprise. And it was done in time, and somehow I've always felt that it completed my cycle. If I consider that I was arrested by the Nazis, and that I finished off putting away 600 of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's quite ironic.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was quite an ironic end to that episode. Unfortunately, in 1957, my late brother committed suicide. He had a retarded child-- he was told by the doctors that the child was retarded. That child is now a woman. She's still alive. She's over 40 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you have contact with her?

speaker: interviewee
text: I keep her in a home, and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think my brother, in a way, was a Holocaust victim. He was nine years old when we left Germany. He had no mother, really, to bring him up. He had no home in which he was brought up. He didn't have the background, he didn't have the strength, to face this tragedy. And when he was finally told by the doctors that there was nothing that could happen to save this child, he waited for me to go out of town on business-- and he was a pharmacist in this country, brilliant brain-- and he took an overdose and killed himself.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1957. February '57.

speaker: interviewer
text: Had your father already died then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, he died in '56, and we had just finished saying kaddish, literally, to the day.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did your father die?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was operated. He had cancer. And that's how I lost my brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have any other member of family anywhere else?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have his-- I have a niece-- that was my brother's second child-- who lives in Johannesburg. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And in 1959, I got married in this country.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you meet your wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: I met her in 1936, when she was 10 years old. And she was a beautiful girl then. And when I came back from the army-- she had married meantime. And, well, that's life-- and I went to-- up the army, and she got married. But subsequently, unbeknown to me, she got divorced. And we met again, and we got married. She had two children from a first marriage, and I brought up those two children. And in 1961, we had a daughter. So I brought up the three children, and now I have three grandchildren. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] But I think that I should say that, talking of these events as I have done, memory changes over 62 years. And human beings also change. My reaction in 1933, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] my fears and my sentiments, are not the memories that I have 62 years later. Nature helps us to forget the worst that we go through. If it didn't, we wouldn't be sane as human beings. And I've always been hesitant to go on record with what happened 62 years ago, because of this aspect-- that I think that is really relevant. And must not be forgotten when you make a testimony of this nature.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you feel about going on record now? Are you pleased you've done it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I think it-- it is necessary to go on record. And I think it's necessary to say that what one survivor said once, that [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what hurt the victims of the Holocaust, or of that period, much more than the cruelty of the oppressor, was the silence of the bystander.

speaker: interviewer
text: Absolutely. Do you think any of that, or your whole experience, affected the way you brought up your children?

speaker: interviewee
text: I hope not. And one of the main reasons why I'm going on record-- why I've gone on record-- is because my children have insisted that they want to know more about my past. And I feel that this way, they have a record of something that both they and my grandchildren should know, even if they don't understand it.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Do you have a message for future generations?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I think that [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we must never forget-- and particularly not as Jews-- that the word kills. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We must be tolerant. We must be one Jewish people. Hitler didn't ask if somebody was orthodox or reform. He didn't ask whether you were observant or not. He classed whether you were a Jew or not. And we haven't learned that lesson. We-- we can see the result of the-- just recent horrific, murder of Rabin. And suddenly, the Jewish people in America, in South Africa, and indeed in Israel, are totally divided. Anybody who's religious is considered a right-winger-- is considered not wanting peace. That's not Jewish, and that's not learning from the past. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] We're-- we're one people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think it could happen again?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't believe the same thing can happen again. But I think we've got a history of 2,000 years of incidents, none as horrible as the Holocaust. But then, also, we must recognize that having lost 6 million people 60 years ago, 62 years ago, [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] we have as many as you want, we've got learned people in Israel. We would never have dreamt that learning could be built as fast as it has been rebuilt. And so the Jewish people has a resilience to build-- but it also has a wastage, if you take the marrying out factor in countries like America, where it's 54%, in England it's 52%, in Switzerland it's 60%. And I believe that it's the education that children get at home-- it's the family life that teaches them to be Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: Absolutely. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] OK. Is there anything else that you want to add as a last kind of thing?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think so.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, well, thank you very much for doing an interview.

speaker: interviewee
text: I hope it comes out all right. That's a photo of my grandfather on my mother's side, taken in Berlin, and his wife, my grandmother, who died many years before him.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name was Julius, and she was Hermine.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember anything about them that you want to bring up?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember that he was a darling-- spent every Sunday going to the cemetery first, to visit his wife's grave. And then he had lunch with us. And then he took me for coffee in a horse-drawn cart.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old was he when he died?

speaker: interviewee
text: He must have been in the 70s. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] But he died before Hitler.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your grandmother?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was a very regal-looking lady. But I-- I don't remember her well. I was perhaps four or five years old when she died. On the right is a photo of my father, which was taken in 1908, and it was taken in Metz, in Elsa's, where he was a buyer in a department store.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old was he at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he was born in '82-- '82-- 1926. He was a good-looking young man, huh? And that's a photo of my mother, as a young girl. It was taken in Berlin-- certainly before she-- before 1913. So she was probably 16 or 17 years old. This is where both my brother and I were born, on the first floor, the left-hand balcony-- although we weren't born on the balcony. But that-- that's the apartment where we were born. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my father told me that [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] when my brother was born, the lights failed, and the doctor screamed. He says, I must have some candles. And he took the Shabbos candles, and he lit them, and my brother was born by the Shabbos candles.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you live in this house for?

speaker: interviewee
text: Until I was five years old, and my brother was one year old. This is a photo of my uncle, who was the brother of my mother, and he's the one who was taken to Dachau. And my father bought him out and had him taken to Palestine.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to him?

speaker: interviewee
text: He died-- he died-- in November in-- on Yom-- on Kol Nidre night, 1959. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Which is the year that I got married. This is a picture of my brother and myself. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Well, it speaks for itself.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would guess about five or six.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you close to your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this taken in Berlin?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This is a picture of the class in 1933. I'm standing to the right of the teacher. And to my right was my best friend then, who lives in Buenos Aires, and who sent me this photograph.

speaker: interviewer
text: What school was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was the Realgymnasium-- Kasir Friedriech Realgymnasium, in Berlin. It was from this class that I was taken. And the three boys to the left of the teacher, were all Hitler Youth. This is a photo of my father, taken in Johannesburg, where he was the treasurer of Our Parent's Home. He was also its founder.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is Our Parent's Home?

speaker: interviewee
text: An old-age home. That's a picture of my late mother, taken in Paris, a year before she died.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's my late wife, who died in 1990-- in 1974. When did you get married?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1959. On the left is my stepdaughter, who lives in Amsterdam. And on the right is my daughter, who lives in Houston, U.S.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was the photo taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: About two years, three years ago. This photo was taken with President Mandela, when I was chairman of the Board of Deputies. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I've known him before he was tried and imprisoned, so our friendship goes back well over 30 years. And when we met the first time after him spending 27 years in prison, he remembered me, and knew exactly where we had met.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where had you met?

speaker: interviewee
text: At-- in Rivonia, at dinner, at Arthur Goldreich's house.
